forms: Present: **am / is / are**; Past Simple: **was / were**; Past Participle: **been**; Present Participle: **being**

definitions.text: to exist or live; to have a specific identity or nature (linking verb): *I think, therefore I am.*
examples: *To be or not to be — that is the question.*
*She was tired after the long journey.*

definitions.text: used as an auxiliary verb to form passive constructions and continuous tenses: *the house was built in 1850.*
examples: *He is running in the park.*
*The letter was written by her.*

definitions.text: to occupy a place or position; to be located: *where have you been all day?*
examples: *I have been to Paris twice.*
*The keys are on the table.*

mainExamples: *I am what I am — nothing more, nothing less.*
*She was so frightened that she could not speak.*
*By the time they arrived, the fire had been burning for hours.*
usageNote: **Be** is the most irregular verb in English, with eight distinct forms (be, am, is, are, was, were, being, been) drawn from three different Old English roots — a phenomenon called suppletion. Contractions are common in speech: *I'm, you're, he's, she's, we're, they're*.

comparisons.description: a more formal synonym for 'to be' in the sense of having existence: *do unicorns exist?* — not used as a linking verb.
indicates a transition into a new state, whereas **be** describes an already existing state: *she became a doctor* vs *she is a doctor*.

idioms.explanation: used to acknowledge a fact while dismissing it as irrelevant to the main point.
the most important element of something; the ultimate purpose or goal.

relatedForms.description: a living creature or entity; the nature or essence of a person.
the past participle of be, used in perfect tenses.

wordHistory: The verb **be** is a suppletion of three Old English roots: *beon*, *wesan*, and *eom/is*. The root *beon* traces to Proto-Indo-European *bhuH-*, cognate with Latin *fui* (I was) and Russian *byt* (to be).
contextStory: Little Mary raised her hand in class. "Teacher, how can one word **be** so many things? I **am**, you **are**, he **is** — and yesterday we all **were**." The teacher smiled: "That is precisely why **be** **is** the most fascinating verb. It **has been** with us since the very beginning of language."

forms: Past Simple: **became**; Past Participle: **become**

definitions.text: to begin to be something; to come to a new state or identity through change: *she worked hard and became a doctor.*
examples: *The caterpillar became a butterfly.*
*It became clear that he was lying.*

definitions.text: (formal/archaic) to be suitable or appropriate for someone; to suit: *such behaviour does not become a gentleman.*
examples: *That dress becomes you wonderfully.*

mainExamples: *He became famous overnight after his first novel was published.*
*What has become of your old friends?*
*Over time, the abandoned house became a ruin.*
usageNote: The fixed phrase **what has become of...?** expresses concern about someone's fate or whereabouts. After **become**, a noun typically appears without an article when referring to a role or title: *he became president* (not *a president*).

comparisons.description: focuses on the end result of a transition: *she became angry* — she is now angry.
more colloquial alternative: *she got angry*. **[[get|Get]]** emphasizes the process of change itself.
often used for unexpected or striking changes: *the milk turned sour.*

relatedForms.description: attractive or suitable for the wearer — derived from the archaic sense of the verb.

wordHistory: Old English *becuman* — 'to arrive, to happen,' from *be-* + *cuman* (to come). Related to German *bekommen* (to receive). The modern sense of 'to come to be' developed in the Middle English period.
contextStory: Nobody believed the quiet boy from the countryside would **become** a champion. Year after year he trained, until the dream **became** real. "How did you **become** so strong?" people asked. He looked at his hands and smiled: "I just decided to **become** who I wanted to be."

forms: Past Simple: **began**; Past Participle: **begun**; Present Participle: **beginning**

definitions.text: to start doing or experiencing something; to come into being: *she began her speech with a quote.*
examples: *The meeting began at nine o'clock.*
*He began to feel uneasy as the hours passed.*

definitions.text: to be the first part or origin of something: *the river begins in the mountains.*
examples: *The trouble began when he refused to apologize.*

mainExamples: *She began her career as a waitress before becoming a famous chef.*
*The war had begun before anyone realized what was happening.*
*Begin as you mean to go on — that's the best advice I ever received.*
usageNote: Both the infinitive (*begin to do*) and gerund (*begin doing*) are acceptable after **begin** with little difference in meaning. However, before *understand* and *realize*, the infinitive is preferred: *I began to understand.*

comparisons.description: a more colloquial and versatile synonym; *start* also means to set a machine in motion (*start the car*), which **begin** does not.
a formal, literary alternative, typical of official documents and ceremonies.

idioms.explanation: firstly; in the initial stage — used to introduce the first point or an early period.
a good start makes the rest of the task easier (proverb).

relatedForms.description: the starting point or first part of something.
someone who is just starting to learn a skill or subject.

wordHistory: Old English *beginnan* — to begin, from *be-* + *ginnan* (to open, to begin). Related to German *beginnen*. The form *began* reflects historical vowel alternation (ablaut).
contextStory: The old maestro sat at the piano and struck the first chord. That was how every concert **began** — with silence turning into sound. He had **begun** learning music at five, crying over scales. Now the hall held its breath: all great things must **begin** with something small.

forms: Past Simple: **bit**; Past Participle: **bitten** (rarely **bit**); Present Participle: **biting**

definitions.text: to use the teeth to cut into or grip something firmly: *the dog bit the postman on the ankle.*
examples: *She bit into the apple eagerly.*
*The frost had bitten the leaves overnight.*

definitions.text: (figurative) to cause pain or discomfort; to have a sharp, unpleasant effect: *the cold wind bit into my face.*
examples: *The new taxes began to bite after six months.*

definitions.text: the act of biting; a wound caused by teeth; a small piece of food: *she took a bite of the sandwich.*
examples: *The dog's bite left a bruise on his arm.*

mainExamples: *He bit his lip to stop himself from crying.*
*She had bitten off more than she could chew by taking on three projects at once.*
*Once bitten, twice shy — he never trusted strangers again after that incident.*
usageNote: The standard past participle is **bitten**; the form *bit* as a past participle is archaic. **Bite the bullet** means to endure something painful. **Bite the hand that feeds you** means to show ingratitude toward a benefactor.

comparisons.description: to grind food with repeated jaw movements — focus on crushing, not on the initial bite.
to bite or chew persistently at something hard; also figurative for persistent worry or anxiety.

idioms.explanation: to endure a painful or difficult situation stoically — from the practice of giving soldiers a bullet to bite during painful procedures.

idioms.phrase: bite off more than you can chew
idioms.explanation: to take on more than one can handle; to overestimate one's abilities.
after a bad experience, a person becomes more cautious.

relatedForms.description: bitingly cold (of wind or frost); sharply critical (of remarks or humor).
injury to body tissue caused by extreme cold — literally 'the frost's bite.'

wordHistory: Old English *bītan* — to bite. Proto-Indo-European root *bheid-* — to split, to pierce. Cognate with Latin *findere* (to split) and German *beißen*.
contextStory: The dog **bit** straight through the mitten — nobody had time to react. The boy yelped and pulled his hand back, staring at the mark where teeth had **bitten** through the wool. His father inspected it calmly: "A clean **bite** — you'll be fine." The boy nodded, eyes wide. He had never **bitten** his own lip so hard as he did right then, trying not to cry.

forms: Past Simple: **broke**; Past Participle: **broken**; Present Participle: **breaking**

definitions.text: to separate into pieces as a result of force; to damage or destroy: *he broke the window with a stone.*
examples: *She dropped the vase and it broke into pieces.*
*The branch broke under his weight.*

definitions.text: to fail to keep a law, rule, or promise: *he broke his promise.*
examples: *You broke the rules — now face the consequences.*

definitions.text: to interrupt or stop the continuity of something: *let's break for lunch.*
examples: *The silence was broken by a distant gunshot.*

definitions.text: a pause in activity; a gap or fracture in something: *take a break.*
examples: *Give me a break!*

mainExamples: *He broke the world record by three seconds.*
*By morning, the fever had broken and she felt better.*
*The news broke that the president had resigned.*
usageNote: The adjective **broken** is widely used: *broken heart*, *broken promise*, *broken English*. **Break** has many phrasal verb forms: *break down* (stop functioning; lose emotional control), *break out* (escape; begin suddenly), *break up* (end a relationship; dissolve), *break in* (interrupt; tame an animal).

comparisons.description: to develop a line or partial split without complete separation — a partial break.
to break violently into many pieces — more forceful and total than **break**.

idioms.explanation: to do something that makes people feel more relaxed in a social situation.
a theatrical expression meaning 'good luck' — based on the superstition that wishing someone luck brings bad fortune.
a decisive situation that will result in either success or failure.

relatedForms.description: damaged and no longer functioning; violated (of a promise); emotionally devastated.
a significant discovery or achievement that breaks through a previous barrier.
a mechanical failure; an emotional collapse; a detailed analysis.

wordHistory: Old English *brecan* — to break. Proto-Indo-European root *bhreg-* — to break, cognate with Latin *frangere* (to break), the source of *fracture*, *fragment*, and *fragile*.
contextStory: The vase fell and **broke** into a hundred pieces. "You **broke** my favourite vase!" gasped grandmother. The cat sat nearby with an expression of complete innocence — it would never admit it **broke** it with its tail. "It **has broken** all by itself," its gaze seemed to say.

forms: Past Simple: **brought**; Past Participle: **brought**

definitions.text: to carry or escort someone or something to a place or person: *bring me the book.*
examples: *She brought flowers to the hospital.*
*He brought his friend to the party.*

definitions.text: to cause something to happen or produce a result: *the storm brought destruction.*
examples: *Money does not always bring happiness.*
*The new law brought many changes.*

definitions.text: to force or persuade oneself to do something unpleasant: *I couldn't bring myself to tell her the truth.*

mainExamples: *Can you bring me a glass of water, please?*
*He brought his laptop to every meeting.*
*The charity has brought hope to thousands of families.*
usageNote: The classic distinction: **bring** (movement toward the speaker) vs **take** (movement away from the speaker). *Bring me the report* (to where I am) vs *take this report to the manager* (away from here).

comparisons.description: to carry something away from the speaker's location: *[[take]] the dog for a walk.*
more colloquial; implies going to get something and bringing it back.

idioms.explanation: to earn money for the family; to be the breadwinner.
to reveal something hidden or previously unknown.

relatedForms.description: the past simple and past participle form of bring.

wordHistory: Old English *bringan* — to bring. The form *brought* reflects historical consonant alternation. Proto-Indo-European root *bher-* — to carry, cognate with Russian *brat* (to take) and Latin *ferre*.
contextStory: Every Sunday, grandfather **brought** the same cherry pie. When he was gone, his grandson **brought** one for the first time himself, following the old recipe with trembling hands. The pie turned out perfectly. He had **brought** more than pastry — he had **brought** his grandfather's memory into the new home.

forms: Past Simple: **built**; Past Participle: **built**

definitions.text: to construct something by joining materials or parts together: *they built a new school in the village.*
examples: *The bridge was built in the nineteenth century.*
*Ants build complex underground colonies.*

definitions.text: to create or develop something gradually over time: *she built her career from scratch.*
examples: *It takes years to build trust.*

definitions.text: the physical structure or physique of a person or animal: *a man of athletic build.*

mainExamples: *Rome wasn't built in a day — great things take time.*
*He built his empire on a foundation of hard work and discipline.*
*Tension had been building for weeks before the confrontation finally happened.*
usageNote: **Build up** means to gradually accumulate or intensify: *build up confidence*, *build up speed*. **Built-in** describes something integrated into a structure from the start.

comparisons.description: to build — more formal and technical, often used for engineering structures.
to raise or put up — literary, typically used for tall structures like monuments or buildings.

idioms.explanation: to have unrealistic dreams or plans; to fantasize about the impossible.

idioms.phrase: Rome wasn't built in a day
idioms.explanation: great achievements require time and effort — they cannot be rushed.

relatedForms.description: a person who constructs buildings.
a structure with walls and a roof; the process of construction.
integrated into a structure or system from the start.

wordHistory: Old English *byldan* — to build, from *bold* — dwelling. The form *built* replaced the earlier *builded* in the 17th century. Proto-Indo-European root *bhel-* — to swell, to grow.
contextStory: Grandfather **built** this house with his own hands in a single summer. His son **built** an extension onto it. Now the grandson **builds** a new storey — with panoramic windows. Three generations, and each has **built** their own part of the same dream.

forms: Past Simple: **bought**; Past Participle: **bought**

definitions.text: to obtain something by paying money for it: *she bought a new dress for the party.*
examples: *He bought the house for a surprisingly low price.*
*I've never bought anything at full price.*

definitions.text: (informal) to believe or accept something as true: *I don't buy that excuse.*
examples: *The detective didn't buy his alibi for a second.*

definitions.text: (informal) a purchase, especially a good bargain: *that car was a great buy.*

mainExamples: *She bought herself a coffee and sat down to think.*
*He had bought the painting long before the artist became famous.*
*You can't buy happiness, but you can buy ice cream — which is almost the same thing.*
usageNote: **Buy** vs **purchase**: *buy* is everyday and colloquial; *purchase* is formal, used in legal documents and business. The phrase **buy time** means to deliberately delay in order to gain more time.

comparisons.description: to buy — formal and legal equivalent, used in contracts, advertising, and business writing.
to obtain — broader meaning: one can acquire not only by paying money but also through effort (skills, habits).

idioms.explanation: to deliberately delay something to gain more time to act or think.
to believe in or accept an idea; also, to acquire a share in a company.

relatedForms.description: a person who buys something; a purchasing agent in commerce.
the purchase of a controlling interest in or all of a company.

wordHistory: Old English *bycgan* — to buy. The form *bought* results from a complex historical alternation (preterite-present verb class). Proto-Indo-European root *bhug-* — to use, to enjoy.
contextStory: He **bought** a lottery ticket out of curiosity. A week later he found out he had **bought** the winning one — a million dollars. "You **bought** happiness!" his friends laughed. He shook his head: "I **bought** only a ticket. Happiness came on its own."

forms: Past Simple: **caught**; Past Participle: **caught**

definitions.text: to seize and hold a moving person, animal, or object: *he caught the ball with one hand.*
examples: *The police caught the thief near the station.*
*She caught the falling vase just in time.*

definitions.text: to reach a vehicle in time to board it: *run or you'll miss the bus.*
examples: *We caught the last train home.*

definitions.text: to contract an illness: *I caught a cold standing in the rain.*
examples: *She caught the flu from her colleague.*

definitions.text: the act of catching; a quantity caught; a hidden disadvantage: *what's the catch?*

mainExamples: *He caught her eye across the room and smiled.*
*The fire had caught by the time the firefighters arrived.*
*I caught myself humming the same song for the third time.*
usageNote: **Catch** has many figurative uses: *catch someone's attention* (attract notice), *catch fire* (begin burning), *catch someone red-handed* (discover in the act). **What's the catch?** asks about a hidden condition or disadvantage.

comparisons.description: to seize suddenly and roughly — a quicker, less precise action than **catch**.
to take someone or something by force — more formal, often used in military or strategic contexts.

idioms.explanation: to discover someone in the act of doing something wrong or illegal.
to reach the same level as someone or something ahead; to make up for lost time.
a situation where escape is impossible because of mutually contradictory conditions — from Joseph Heller's novel.

relatedForms.description: a person or device that catches; in baseball, the fielder behind home plate.
infectious or contagious — used of illnesses and also figuratively of laughter or enthusiasm.

wordHistory: Middle English *cacchen* — to chase, to seize, from Old French *cachier* (to hunt), from Latin *captiare* — to catch. Related to Latin *capere* (to take), the source of *capture*, *captive*, and *capable*.
contextStory: The fisherman **caught** an enormous carp at dawn. "I **caught** it!" he shouted. But the carp **caught** him off guard — with one powerful thrash it broke free and splashed back into the water. Now he has **caught** only his neighbours' laughter, though the story grows larger with every telling.

forms: Past Simple: **chose**; Past Participle: **chosen**; Present Participle: **choosing**

definitions.text: to pick or select from a number of alternatives: *she chose the red dress over the blue one.*
examples: *He was chosen to lead the team.*
*You must choose between staying and leaving.*

definitions.text: to decide to do something; to prefer: *I chose to stay silent.*
examples: *She chose not to tell him the truth.*

mainExamples: *He spent an hour in the store before choosing the right gift.*
*I've chosen you — out of everyone, it's you.*
*The jury has chosen not to comment on the verdict.*
usageNote: Common confusion: **choose** (present) vs **chose** (past). Remember the three forms: *choose / chose / chosen*. **Pick** is a more casual synonym. Common structures: *choose between A and B*, *choose from a list*, *choose to do something*.

comparisons.description: to choose — more formal and deliberate, implying careful evaluation of criteria.
to choose — casual and spontaneous, less deliberate than **choose**.
to choose by vote — usually for political or official positions.

idioms.explanation: a small, privileged group selected from a larger number.
to select only the best options while rejecting the rest; to be very selective.

relatedForms.description: an act of choosing; the thing that is chosen; the right or ability to choose.
selected or preferred — past participle used as an adjective.

wordHistory: Old English *ceosan* — to choose. Proto-Indo-European root *ĝeus-* — to taste, to try. Cognate with German *kiesen* and Latin *gustare* (to taste) — the source of *gusto* and *disgust*.
contextStory: The menu was thirty pages long. He **chose** the soup — and immediately regretted it. His neighbour **chose** the steak, and it looked magnificent. When the waiter asked him again, he thought long and hard: life is short, so one must **choose** wisely. In the end, he **has chosen** dessert — because dessert never disappoints.

forms: Past Simple: **came**; Past Participle: **come**; Present Participle: **coming**

definitions.text: to move toward or arrive at a place near the speaker or a mentioned location: *come here, please.*
examples: *She came home late that evening.*
*Spring comes every year, no matter how long the winter.*

definitions.text: to happen; to occur; to arrive in time: *change doesn't come easily.*
examples: *The moment of truth has come.*

definitions.text: to reach a particular state or result: *it came loose.*
examples: *Everything will come right in the end.*

mainExamples: *He came running when he heard the news.*
*The answer came to her in the middle of the night.*
*She has come a long way since her first performance.*
usageNote: **Come** vs **go**: *come* indicates movement toward the speaker or the place being discussed; *go* indicates movement away. However, when projecting yourself to the listener's location (*I'll come to your party*), *come* is still used. Numerous phrasal verbs: *come across* (encounter), *come back* (return), *come up with* (invent), *come out* (emerge, be published).

comparisons.description: to move away from the speaker or toward a distant place.
to reach a destination — emphasizes the completion of travel, more formal than *come*.

idioms.explanation: whatever happens; regardless of circumstances.
now that I think about it — introduces a thought that occurs in mid-conversation.
to reach adulthood; to mature and become independent.

relatedForms.description: money received regularly — literally 'that which comes in.'
a result or consequence — literally 'that which comes out.'
to greet; a greeting — from *well* + *come*.

wordHistory: Old English *cuman* — to come. Proto-Indo-European root *gwa-* — to go, to come. Cognate with German *kommen*, Latin *venire* (the source of *advent* and *convention*), and Greek *bainein*.
contextStory: She **came** home to find the table set — candles, flowers, favourite dishes. "Where did all this come from?" she gasped. "The idea **came** to me this morning," he smiled. "I knew you **had come** to the end of a hard week. I wanted Friday to **come** with a surprise."

forms: Past Simple: **cut**; Past Participle: **cut** (все три формы одинаковы: cut – cut – cut)

definitions.text: to divide or penetrate something with a sharp tool: *she cut the bread into slices.*
examples: *He cut his finger while chopping vegetables.*
*The surgeon cut carefully along the marked line.*

definitions.text: to reduce in amount, size, or number: *the company cut its budget by thirty percent.*
examples: *We need to cut unnecessary expenses.*

definitions.text: a wound made by a sharp object; a reduction; a share: *a cut in salary.*
examples: *She had a small cut on her hand.*

mainExamples: *He cut the rope and set the prisoner free.*
*The new road has cut the journey time in half.*
*The diamond has been cut to perfection.*
usageNote: **Cut** is one of the few completely invariable irregular verbs: V1=V2=V3=**cut**. Phrasal verbs: *cut down* (reduce; fell), *cut off* (sever; interrupt), *cut out* (remove by cutting; quit), *cut back* (reduce spending or production).

comparisons.description: to cut into thin, flat pieces — more specific than **cut**.
to cut roughly with heavy blows — typically used for wood or coarse food preparation.
to remove unwanted edges neatly — a more delicate and precise action than **cut**.

idioms.explanation: to do something the easy or cheap way, sacrificing quality or thoroughness.
to skip the preamble and get to the important point immediately.
already decided or settled; leaving no room for discussion.

relatedForms.description: a tool or person that cuts.
sharply cold or hurtful (of wind or remarks); a clipping from a plant or newspaper.

wordHistory: Middle English *cutten* — of obscure origin; possibly from a Scandinavian dialect word *kuta* — to cut with a knife. No clear Proto-Indo-European cognates among major European languages.
contextStory: The chef **cut** vegetables with such speed that the knife was almost invisible. He **cut** the carrot, **cut** the onion, **cut** the pepper — all in under a minute. "Have you ever **cut** yourself?" asked the apprentice. "Never," replied the chef without looking up. "Those who truly understand a blade do not **cut** themselves."

forms: Present: **do / does** (3rd person); Past Simple: **did**; Past Participle: **done**; Present Participle: **doing**

definitions.text: to perform, execute, or carry out an action or task: *what are you doing?*
examples: *She does her homework every evening.*
*He did his best to help.*

definitions.text: auxiliary verb used to form questions and negatives in Present and Past Simple: *do you like music?*
examples: *I don't understand the problem.*
*Did she call yesterday?*

definitions.text: to be adequate or acceptable: *this room will do.*
examples: *Will ten dollars do?*

mainExamples: *Just do it — stop thinking and act.*
*What have you done to your hair?*
*The damage had already been done by the time we arrived.*
usageNote: **Do** is one of the most important verbs in English. As an auxiliary it forms questions (*Do you...? Did she...?*) and negatives (*don't, doesn't, didn't*). As a main verb it means to perform or execute. **Done!** as an exclamation means 'agreed!' or 'finished!'

comparisons.description: to create something new: *[[make]] a cake, make a mistake, make a decision* — unlike **do**, **make** implies producing a product or result.
to carry out — more formal, used for tasks, duties, and public performances.

idioms.explanation: a situation of extreme risk where there is no middle ground — complete the task or face total failure.
something that sounds simple but is much harder in practice.
to abolish or get rid of something.

relatedForms.description: finished; cooked sufficiently; socially unacceptable (as in 'not done').
a person who acts rather than just talks; an active, practical person.
an act or action — an archaic/literary form related to the verb *do*.

wordHistory: Old English *don* — to do, to make. Proto-Indo-European root *dʰeh₁-* — to set, to put. Cognate with Latin *facere* (to make, the source of *fact*, *factory*, *fiction*) and Greek *tithenai* (to place).
contextStory: "What are you **doing** all morning?" asked his mother. "Doing things," he replied vaguely. An hour later she walked in: he **did** a full spring clean of the entire house. "Are you **done**?" she gasped. "Not yet," he smiled, "but I've **done** the most important part — I wanted you to be proud."

forms: Past Simple: **drew**; Past Participle: **drawn**; Present Participle: **drawing**

definitions.text: to make a picture or diagram with a pencil or pen: *she drew a portrait of her grandmother.*
examples: *He drew a map on a napkin.*
*Children love to draw animals.*

definitions.text: to pull or extract something: *draw a sword from its scabbard.*
examples: *She drew a deep breath before speaking.*
*He drew water from the well.*

definitions.text: to attract or bring toward: *the exhibition drew large crowds.*
examples: *The smell of fresh bread drew customers in.*

definitions.text: a tied game; a random selection; something that attracts people: *the match ended in a draw.*

mainExamples: *He drew his conclusions from years of careful observation.*
*The curtains were drawn, and the room was dark.*
*She has drawn inspiration from her travels.*
usageNote: **Draw** has a wide range of meanings. **Draw a conclusion** means to infer. **Draw the line** means to set a limit. Note: **draw** (using a pencil or pen) vs **paint** (using brush and pigment).

comparisons.description: to make a quick, rough drawing without fine detail — a preliminary drawing.
to create images with brush and paint — distinct from drawing with pencil or pen.

idioms.explanation: to set a limit; to refuse to go further than a certain point.
to start over completely after a failure, returning to the planning stage.
to fail to remember something; to get no result from a search.

relatedForms.description: a picture made with a pencil or pen; the act or skill of making such pictures.
a sliding compartment in a piece of furniture; a person who draws.
a disadvantage or problem — literally something that pulls you back.

wordHistory: Old English *dragan* — to pull, to drag. Proto-Indo-European root *dhragh-* — to pull. Cognate with German *tragen* (to carry) and Latin *trahere* (to pull), the source of *tractor*, *attract*, and *contract*.
contextStory: The artist **drew** the stranger sitting by the cafe window. The man noticed and came over: "You **drew** me?" he asked. "May I look?" The face on the paper seemed more alive than any mirror. "You have **drawn** me better than I am," the stranger said quietly. The artist shook his head: "I **drew** only what I saw."

forms: Past Simple: **drank**; Past Participle: **drunk**; Present Participle: **drinking**

definitions.text: to take liquid into the mouth and swallow it: *she drank a glass of water.*
examples: *He drinks coffee every morning.*
*The horse drank from the river.*

definitions.text: to consume alcohol: *he drinks too much.*
examples: *She hasn't drunk alcohol in ten years.*

definitions.text: a liquid consumed; a portion of alcohol; the act of drinking: *can I offer you a drink?*

mainExamples: *He drank the whole bottle before I noticed.*
*We had drunk our fill and were ready to leave.*
*Drink to me only with thine eyes.* (Ben Jonson)
usageNote: Do not confuse **drunk** as a past participle (*I have drunk*) with **drunk** as an adjective (intoxicated). The form **drunken** is used only attributively before a noun: *a drunken brawl*, not *he was drunken*.

comparisons.description: to drink in small amounts, slowly and carefully.
to swallow quickly in large mouthfuls — the opposite of sip.

idioms.explanation: to raise a glass in honour of a person or occasion; to toast.
to consume very large quantities of alcohol habitually.

relatedForms.description: intoxicated by alcohol.
safe or pleasant enough to drink.

wordHistory: Old English *drincan* — to drink. Proto-Indo-European root *dhreg-* — to pull, possibly linked to the idea of drawing liquid. Cognate with German *trinken*.
contextStory: He **drank** greedily after the marathon — one cup, then another. By evening he **had drunk** three litres and still felt thirsty. "Keep **drinking**," said the doctor. He **drank** obediently, and slowly the world stopped spinning. A body that **has drunk** everything it has will **drink** whatever it can find to come back to life.

forms: Past Simple: **drove**; Past Participle: **driven**; Present Participle: **driving**

definitions.text: to operate and control a vehicle: *she drives to work every day.*
examples: *He drove across the country in three days.*
*Have you ever driven a truck?*

definitions.text: to compel, motivate, or force someone into an action or state: *hunger drove him to steal.*
examples: *What drives you to succeed?*
*The noise is driving me crazy.*

definitions.text: a journey in a vehicle; inner motivation or determination; a private road to a house: *a scenic drive through the mountains.*

mainExamples: *She drove through the night to be there for her friend.*
*He has driven that same route for twenty years.*
*Ambition and curiosity drive the greatest discoveries.*
usageNote: Figurative uses: *drive someone crazy/mad* — to infuriate; *what drives you?* — what motivates you? **Driven** as an adjective means intensely motivated: *a driven person*. **Drive** as a noun also refers to a computer storage device (*hard drive*).

comparisons.description: to travel on a bicycle, horse, motorcycle, or as a passenger — unlike **drive**, it does not imply controlling a car.
to guide the direction of a vehicle — a specific physical action that is part of driving.

idioms.explanation: to irritate or frustrate someone intensely.
to emphasize something very forcefully so it is clearly understood.
what are you trying to say or imply?

relatedForms.description: a person who drives; a motivating force.
highly motivated and ambitious; compelled by strong internal forces.

wordHistory: Old English *drīfan* — to drive, to chase. Proto-Indo-European root *dhreibh-* — to push. Cognate with German *treiben* (to drive, to propel). Originally used of herding animals, then extended to vehicles.
contextStory: She **drove** for eight hours without stopping. In the rearview mirror — only darkness. Exhaustion **drove** her to the shoulder, but the thought of her mother in hospital **drove** her on. By dawn, she **had driven** through three states. She **drives** not with a steering wheel — she **drives** with love.

forms: Past Simple: **ate**; Past Participle: **eaten**; Present Participle: **eating**

definitions.text: to put food in the mouth, chew, and swallow it: *she ate the whole pizza by herself.*
examples: *We eat together as a family every Sunday.*
*He ate quickly and went back to work.*

definitions.text: (figurative) to gradually destroy or consume: *rust had eaten through the metal.*
examples: *Guilt was eating him alive.*

mainExamples: *We ate in silence, neither of us knowing what to say.*
*She has eaten nothing since yesterday morning.*
*The company's losses ate into its reserves.*
usageNote: In American English *ate* rhymes with *late*; in British English it traditionally rhymes with *let* [et]. **Eat out** — to dine at a restaurant. **Eat up** — to finish everything on the plate. **What's eating you?** means 'what's bothering you?'

comparisons.description: to eat greedily, quickly, and completely; also figurative for consuming books or information.
to eat in small, cautious bites — tentatively or without much appetite.

idioms.explanation: to be forced to admit that something you said was wrong.
to be completely under someone's control; to be docile and obedient.

idioms.phrase: have one's cake and eat it
idioms.explanation: to want two incompatible things at the same time; to have the benefit of both sides.

relatedForms.description: a person who eats in a particular way: *a picky eater.*
safe and suitable to eat — from the related Latin *edere*.

wordHistory: Old English *etan* — to eat. Proto-Indo-European root *h₁ed-* — to eat. Cognate with Latin *edere* (the source of *edible*), German *essen*, and Greek *edein*.
contextStory: He **ate** breakfast standing up, as always. A year later, when she was gone, he understood: they had never truly **eaten** together. Now he **eats** slowly, at a properly set table, and the taste has **eaten** something new into him — the desire to be present.

forms: Past Simple: **fell**; Past Participle: **fallen**; Present Participle: **falling**

definitions.text: to move downward under the force of gravity: *the apple fell from the tree.*
examples: *She slipped and fell on the ice.*
*Snow had fallen during the night.*

definitions.text: to decrease in quantity or level: *temperatures fell below zero.*
examples: *Sales have fallen sharply this quarter.*

definitions.text: to enter a particular state: *she fell asleep immediately.*
examples: *He fell ill just before the exam.*
*They fell in love on the first trip.*

definitions.text: the act of falling; a decrease; (American English) autumn: *the fall of the Roman Empire.*

mainExamples: *He fell silent when she entered the room.*
*The city had fallen to the enemy by dawn.*
*Leaves were falling one by one in the autumn breeze.*
usageNote: **Fall** (AmE) = **autumn** (BrE). **Fall** + adjective = to enter a state: *fall asleep, fall ill, fall silent, fall in love*. **Fall** vs **drop**: *fall* is typically uncontrolled; *drop* can be intentional.

comparisons.description: to fall or let fall — can be deliberate (to drop a hint) or accidental (to drop a plate).
to fall suddenly with a rolling or spinning motion; to collapse in a disorganized way.

idioms.explanation: to be ignored or disregarded — said of words or appeals that nobody heeds.
to lose one's good reputation or the favour of others.
to fail to produce the intended effect — said of a joke, plan, or performance.

relatedForms.description: having fallen; killed in battle; morally degraded.
a loss of power or status; the cause of someone's ruin.
a cascade of water falling from a height — literally 'water that falls.'

wordHistory: Old English *feallan* — to fall. Proto-Indo-European root *phol-* or *sphal-* — to fall, to stumble. Cognate with German *fallen*.
contextStory: He **fell** from the ladder and broke his arm. While he lay in hospital, his reputation **fell** too — the boss had learned the truth. By spring, everything he had built **had fallen** to ruin. But it was only then that he understood: sometimes you need to **fall** completely before you can truly rise.

forms: Past Simple: **felt**; Past Participle: **felt**

definitions.text: to experience an emotion or physical sensation: *she felt the warmth of the sun.*
examples: *He felt nervous before the interview.*
*I felt a sharp pain in my side.*

definitions.text: to have an opinion or belief, especially based on intuition: *I feel that something is wrong.*
examples: *She felt it was the right decision.*

definitions.text: to touch or explore by touch; (of a surface) to have a particular texture: *the fabric feels soft.*
examples: *He felt his way through the dark corridor.*

definitions.text: a sensation or atmosphere; the texture perceived by touch: *the feel of silk against her skin.*

mainExamples: *He felt like a stranger in his own home.*
*She has never felt so alive as she did that day.*
*The whole city felt different after the rain.*
usageNote: **Feel** + adjective describes a state: *feel tired, feel happy, feel sick*. **Feel like** + noun/gerund = to want: *I feel like pizza*, *I feel like crying*. **Get a feel for** = to become familiar with something.

comparisons.description: to perceive intuitively — more vague and uncertain than **feel**.
to make physical contact with a surface — the physical act that **feel** describes as a sensation.

idioms.explanation: to know something intuitively without concrete evidence.
to feel uncomfortable or not belonging in a situation or environment.

relatedForms.description: an emotion, sensation, or intuitive sense.
a type of compressed fabric (homonym — unrelated to the verb).

wordHistory: Old English *felan* — to touch, to perceive. Proto-Indo-European root *pal-* — to touch, to feel. Cognate with German *fühlen*.
contextStory: She **felt** his gaze before she even turned around. When she did, he **felt** something warm bloom in his chest. Years passed, but they both **have felt** that moment their entire lives. "Do you remember how we **felt** it?" he asked one day. She nodded. No words were needed.

forms: Past Simple: **found**; Past Participle: **found**

definitions.text: to discover something by searching or by chance: *she found her keys under the sofa.*
examples: *He found a wallet on the street.*
*Scientists have found a cure for the disease.*

definitions.text: to perceive or consider something to have a particular quality: *I found the book very interesting.*
examples: *She found it difficult to concentrate.*
*He found himself alone in the city.*

definitions.text: a valuable or interesting discovery: *the archaeological find of the century.*

mainExamples: *He found the courage to tell the truth.*
*By the time they found her, she had been missing for three days.*
*Find what you love and let it kill you.* (attr. G.B. Shaw)
usageNote: **Find** vs **look for**: *look for* describes the search process; *find* describes the result. *I'm looking for my keys* vs *I found my keys*. **Find out** = to discover a fact or piece of information. **Find fault with** = to criticize.

comparisons.description: to find something new or important that was previously unknown — more significant than a simple **find**.
to determine the position of something — more precise and technical than **find**.

idioms.explanation: to become comfortable and capable in a new situation; to get established.
to manage to feel compassion or forgiveness for someone.

relatedForms.description: a conclusion or result of research or investigation.
a person who finds something; a device that locates something.

wordHistory: Old English *findan* — to find, to discover. Proto-Indo-European root *pent-* — to go, to travel (the sense being 'to arrive at what you sought'). Cognate with German *finden*.
contextStory: She **found** an envelope inside an old book — yellowed, with her name on it. She **found** a letter from her grandmother, written twenty years ago. "If you have **found** this letter, then you are already the person I hoped you would become." She **has found** far more than a letter — she **found** herself.

forms: Past Simple: **flew**; Past Participle: **flown**; Present Participle: **flying**

definitions.text: to move through the air, typically with wings: *birds fly south in winter.*
examples: *She has flown over the Atlantic three times.*
*He flew from London to Tokyo in twelve hours.*

definitions.text: to control and pilot an aircraft: *she flew the helicopter into the storm.*
to pass very quickly (especially of time): *time flies when you're having fun.*
examples: *The weekend flew by.*

definitions.text: a small flying insect: *a fly buzzed around the room.*

mainExamples: *The eagle flew so high it became a speck in the sky.*
*He had never flown before and was terrified.*
*Let your imagination fly.*
usageNote: Do not confuse the verb *fly – flew – flown* (to travel through air) with the noun *fly* (an insect). **Fly off the handle** = to suddenly lose one's temper. **On the fly** = while moving; without preparation.

comparisons.description: to fly high effortlessly and gracefully — often used of birds or inspiring things.
to fly smoothly without flapping wings — effortless, silent movement through air.

idioms.explanation: time passes quickly — often used with surprise at how fast time has passed.
to suddenly and intensely lose one's temper.
an unseen observer — someone who wishes to watch events without being noticed.

relatedForms.description: the act of flying; a journey by air; an act of fleeing.
a pilot; a leaflet or advertisement distributed to people.

wordHistory: Old English *flēogan* — to fly. Proto-Indo-European root *pleu-* — to flow, to swim, to fly. Cognate with German *fliegen*. Flies (the insects) were named for their ability to fly.
contextStory: He **flew** from Moscow to Tokyo without a stopover. As a child he had dreamed of flying — and now he **flew**. Looking down at the clouds, he remembered the day a paper plane **flew** the farthest of anyone's in class. Something **had flown** out of him that day and only now, at thirty thousand feet, **has** it **flown** back.

forms: Past Simple: **forgot**; Past Participle: **forgotten** (AmE also **forgot**); Present Participle: **forgetting**

definitions.text: to fail to remember something: *I forgot her name immediately after we met.*
examples: *He forgot to lock the door.*
*She had forgotten where she put her glasses.*

definitions.text: to deliberately stop thinking about something; to put aside: *forget it, it doesn't matter.*
examples: *Try to forget about your problems for a while.*

mainExamples: *I forgot your birthday — I'm so sorry.*
*She has forgotten more about cooking than most people ever learn.*
*Forget what you know and open your mind.*
usageNote: Important distinction: **forget to do** (failed to do it) vs **forget doing** (did it but have no memory): *I forgot to call her* (didn't call) vs *I forgot calling her* (called but don't remember). **Forget it!** means 'never mind' or 'you're welcome.'

comparisons.description: to recall something from memory — the direct opposite of **forget**.
to fail to notice or consider something — often unintentional, but not about memory loss.

idioms.explanation: to lose control of one's behaviour; to act in an inappropriate way.
to pardon someone completely and let go of all resentment.

relatedForms.description: having a tendency to forget things frequently.
so remarkable or moving that it cannot be forgotten.

wordHistory: Old English *forgietan* — to forget, from *for-* (away, to lose) + *gietan* (to grasp). Literally 'to lose one's grip on something in the mind.'
contextStory: He **forgot** her phone number the moment he stepped out of the cafe. The next day he **forgot** her face too. But the scent of her perfume — never. That one thing **has** never **forgotten** him. Sometimes the mind **forgets** what matters, but the body remembers what the mind can never truly **forget**.

forms: Past Simple: **got**; Past Participle: **got** (BrE) / **gotten** (AmE); Present Participle: **getting**

definitions.text: to obtain, receive, or acquire something: *can I get you a drink?*
examples: *She got a letter from her brother.*
*Where did you get that idea?*

definitions.text: to come to be in a particular state; to become: *it's getting cold.*
examples: *He got angry when he heard the news.*
*She got married last summer.*

definitions.text: to understand something: *do you get it?*
examples: *I don't get the joke.*

definitions.text: to arrive at or reach a place: *how do I get to the station?*

mainExamples: *Get up — we'll be late!*
*She got what she deserved.*
*He has gotten better since starting the treatment.* (AmE) / *He has got better.* (BrE)
usageNote: **Get** is one of the most versatile verbs in English. In British English the past participle is **got** (*I've got a car* = I own a car). In American English it is **gotten** (*I've gotten a new car* = I acquired one). **Have got** (BrE) = **have** (possession). Key phrasal verbs: *get up, get on, get off, get through, get over, get away*.

comparisons.description: to acquire something — a more formal synonym for the 'receive/acquire' sense of **get**.
to come to be — a more formal synonym for the 'change of state' sense of **get**.

idioms.explanation: to escape punishment or consequences for something wrong.
to recover from an illness, loss, or difficult experience.
to learn how to do something; to become familiar with something.

relatedForms.description: American English past participle of get; archaic in British English.
an escape; a short holiday or trip.

wordHistory: Middle English *geten*, from Old Norse *geta* — to obtain, to understand. Proto-Indo-European root *ghend-* — to seize. Cognate with Latin *praehendere* (to grasp), the source of *comprehend* and *apprehend*.
contextStory: He **got** the job of his dreams. In his excitement he **got** cake and flowers. By evening he **got** another call: the job was in another country. Just as he thought he had **gotten** everything, life reminded him: sometimes to **get** one thing, you must let go of another.

forms: Past Simple: **gave**; Past Participle: **given**; Present Participle: **giving**

definitions.text: to hand something over to someone; to provide or present: *she gave him a book for his birthday.*
examples: *He gave all his money to charity.*
*Can you give me a hand?*

definitions.text: to produce or cause a feeling or result: *the music gives me joy.*
examples: *This medicine will give you relief.*

definitions.text: to yield or bend under pressure: *the branch gave under his weight.*

mainExamples: *Give it your best shot.*
*She had given up everything for this chance.*
*He gives generously to everyone around him.*
usageNote: **Give away** = to give for free, or to reveal a secret unintentionally. **Given** as a preposition: *given the circumstances* = considering the situation. **Give or take** = approximately, plus or minus.

comparisons.description: to give to a charity or cause — more formal and purposeful than a simple **give**.
to officially give or allow — used for rights, permissions, and subsidies.

idioms.explanation: mutual compromise and exchange; the practice of making concessions in a relationship.
to die; also (of a machine) to stop working permanently.

idioms.phrase: give the benefit of the doubt
idioms.explanation: to choose to believe the best of someone when the truth is uncertain.

relatedForms.description: specified or stated; (as a preposition) considering: *given the cost...*
a person who gives, especially one known for generosity.

wordHistory: Old English *giefan* — to give. Proto-Indo-European root *gʰebʰ-* — to give, to receive. Cognate with German *geben* and possibly Latin *habere* (to have).
contextStory: She **gave** her umbrella to a stranger at the metro — a rainy Tuesday, no hesitation. He **gave** her a look of pure astonishment. A year later, at the same spot, he found her and **gave** her the umbrella back — repaired and tied with a red ribbon. Life **has given** them a second chance, because once someone decided to **give** without expecting anything in return.

forms: Present (3rd): **goes**; Past Simple: **went**; Past Participle: **gone**; Present Participle: **going**

definitions.text: to move from one place to another, especially away from the speaker: *she goes to school every day.*
examples: *He went to Paris last year.*
*The train has gone — we missed it.*

definitions.text: to disappear or come to an end: *the pain finally went away.*
examples: *My headache has gone.*

definitions.text: to work or function: *the car won't go.*
examples: *Is the heating going?*

definitions.text: an attempt; a turn: *have a go.*

mainExamples: *Here we go — the adventure begins!*
*Everything went wrong from the very start.*
*She has gone further than anyone thought possible.*
usageNote: **Go** vs **come**: *go* = movement away from the speaker; *come* = movement toward. **Be gone** vs **have gone**: *He is gone* = he has disappeared/died; *He has gone to Paris* = he went there and is still there. **Go + -ing** describes activities: *go swimming, go shopping, go dancing*.

comparisons.description: to move toward the speaker or a mentioned location — the opposite direction of **go**.
to depart from a place — focuses on the departure point, not the destination.

idioms.explanation: said just before starting something risky or uncertain — a declaration of readiness.
to make more effort than is required or expected.
to conflict with natural instincts or with what is generally accepted.

relatedForms.description: no longer present; departed; dead (in informal speech).
socially confident and friendly; leaving a position or role.

wordHistory: A remarkable case of suppletion: the past tense **went** comes from a completely different Old English verb — *wendan* (to go, to turn). **Go** itself comes from Old English *gan*. The two verbs merged into a single paradigm.
contextStory: He walked to the door and stopped. "If I **go** now, I won't come back," he said. She said nothing. He **went** anyway. Three years later, having **gone** through all of it, he understood: sometimes you need to **go** to discover where you actually want to arrive.

forms: Past Simple: **grew**; Past Participle: **grown**; Present Participle: **growing**

definitions.text: to increase in size, number, or degree; to develop: *children grow fast.*
examples: *The tree has grown three metres in five years.*
*She grew up in a small village.*

definitions.text: to cultivate plants: *he grows vegetables in his garden.*
to gradually become a particular state: *she grew tired of waiting.*
examples: *He grew bolder with each attempt.*

mainExamples: *The company grew from a small startup into a global corporation.*
*He grew a beard during the lockdown.*
*Her confidence has grown enormously this year.*
usageNote: **Grow up** = to mature from a child into an adult. **Grow on** = to become more appealing over time: *this song grew on me*. **Outgrow** = to become too large or too mature for something.

comparisons.description: to evolve or advance gradually — a broader term that includes planned improvement.
to increase in size, scope, or volume — often used for businesses or territories.

idioms.explanation: to become more pleasing over time — said of something initially disliked.
to become too big for clothes; to abandon a habit or behaviour as one matures.

relatedForms.description: the process of increasing or developing; a tumour or abnormal mass.
an adult; mature and responsible.
covered with plants that have grown beyond control.

wordHistory: Old English *growan* — to grow. Proto-Indo-European root *ghreh₁-* — to grow, to become green. Cognate with German *grünen* (to turn green) and likely related to *green* itself.
contextStory: Grandfather planted an apple tree the year his grandson was born. The tree **grew** slowly, just as the boy did. When the boy **grew up**, the tree was already bearing fruit. "It **grew** alongside me," he said. And it was true: they both **have grown** from the same seed — from an old man's love for the future.

forms: Past Simple: **hung**; Past Participle: **hung** (для предметов) — Past Simple: **hanged**; Past Participle: **hanged** (о смертной казни через повешение)

definitions.text: to attach from above; to be suspended from a point: *she hung the painting on the wall.*
examples: *The laundry hung on the line in the wind.*
*He hung his coat on the door.*

definitions.text: to execute by suspension from the neck (forms: hanged/hanged only): *the murderer was hanged in 1875.*
(of a computer program) to stop responding and freeze: *my computer keeps hanging.*

mainExamples: *Dark clouds hung over the valley all morning.*
*He hung on to the rope with all his strength.*
*The question has hung unanswered for years.*
usageNote: The key distinction: **hung** is used for objects (*hung the picture, hung the curtains*); **hanged** is used only for execution by hanging. The rule: 'People are hanged; pictures are hung.' **Hang around** = to loiter. **Hang on** = wait; to hold tightly.

comparisons.description: to hang from above — more formal; also means to temporarily stop something.
to hang loosely and swing from side to side.

idioms.explanation: to keep going in a difficult situation; don't give up.
to be in extreme danger or uncertainty; barely surviving.
to learn how to do something properly; to become familiar with something.

relatedForms.description: a device for hanging clothes; historically, a gallows.
to project outward over something; a projecting part of a structure.

wordHistory: Two separate Old English verbs merged into one: *hangian* (to be suspended, intransitive) and *hon* (to cause to hang, transitive). This is why two past-tense forms exist. Proto-Indo-European root *konk-* — to hang.
contextStory: The artist **hung** a canvas on the empty wall and stepped back. The whole room transformed. For three weeks it **hung** there in silence, until one morning he realized: it was the best thing he had ever made. It **has hung** there to this day — and everyone who enters freezes, as if they too **hang** in the air for a moment.

forms: Present (3rd): **has**; Past Simple: **had**; Past Participle: **had**; Present Participle: **having**

definitions.text: to own or possess something: *she has a dog.*
examples: *He had a lot of money when he was young.*
*Do you have any questions?*

definitions.text: auxiliary verb used to form perfect tenses: *she has finished her work.*
examples: *I have been waiting for an hour.*
*Had he known, he would have acted differently.*

definitions.text: to experience or perform an action: *have a meal; have a conversation.*
examples: *We had a great time at the party.*

mainExamples: *I have never had such a good cup of coffee.*
*She had the courage to say what nobody else would.*
*Have you had a chance to read the report?*
usageNote: **Have** is the second most important auxiliary after **be**. **Have to** = must (obligation). British **have got** = American **have** (possession). Negatives: *haven't, hasn't, hadn't*. Contracted forms: *I've, she's, he'd, they'd*.

comparisons.description: to possess — more formal and limited to property; cannot express experience.
to own or have — literary; used for qualities, characteristics, and property.

idioms.explanation: to openly confront someone and discuss grievances directly.
to be completely exhausted; to be in an inescapable situation.
to thoroughly enjoy an opportunity, especially at someone else's expense.

relatedForms.description: the state of owning or possessing something.

wordHistory: Old English *habban* — to have, to hold. Proto-Indo-European root *kap-* — to grasp, to hold. Cognate with Latin *capere* (to take, the source of *capture* and *capable*) and German *haben*.
contextStory: She **had** everything: a career, an apartment, friends. But one morning she realised: she **had** everything except time for herself. Now she **has** fewer things — and more life. Sometimes the most valuable thing you can **have** is an empty evening that belongs only to you.

forms: Past Simple: **heard**; Past Participle: **heard**

definitions.text: to perceive sounds with the ears: *I can hear music from next door.*
examples: *She heard footsteps in the corridor.*
*He had heard the news on the radio.*

definitions.text: to be informed about something; to receive news: *have you heard from John?*
examples: *I heard that she got married.*

definitions.text: to listen to and consider officially (of a court or judge): *the judge heard the case.*

mainExamples: *He heard the door slam and knew she was gone.*
*I've never heard anything so beautiful in my life.*
*Hear me out before you judge.*
usageNote: **Hear** vs **listen**: *hear* is passive (sound enters the ear without effort); *listen* is active and deliberate. *I hear the rain* vs *I'm listening to music*. **Hear, hear!** = an exclamation of agreement and approval at meetings.

comparisons.description: to pay active, deliberate attention to a sound — the opposite of passive *hear*.
to accidentally hear a conversation not intended for you.

idioms.explanation: an exclamation of agreement and approval — used in formal meetings and parliament.
a categorical refusal to even consider a suggestion or proposal.

relatedForms.description: the sense of perceiving sounds; a formal session in court.
rumour or unverified information obtained from others — second-hand knowledge.

wordHistory: Old English *hīeran* — to hear. Proto-Indo-European root *h₂kous-* — to perceive by ear. Cognate with German *hören* and Greek *akouein* (the source of *acoustic*).
contextStory: She **heard** his voice for the first time over the phone — and froze. Nothing remarkable was said, but something in that voice she **heard** even before the words. Later, years on, as he read to her aloud, she would close her eyes: she **has heard** a thousand voices, but only this one she **hears** with her heart.

forms: Past Simple: **hid**; Past Participle: **hidden** (rarely **hid**); Present Participle: **hiding**

definitions.text: to put something in a place where it cannot be easily found: *she hid the letter under the mattress.*
examples: *The child hid behind the curtain.*
*He has hidden the key somewhere in the garden.*

definitions.text: to conceal feelings or information: *she tried to hide her disappointment.*
examples: *You can't hide the truth forever.*

definitions.text: an animal skin; a camouflaged shelter for watching wildlife: *a bird hide.*

mainExamples: *He hid his fear behind a confident smile.*
*She had been hidden in plain sight the whole time.*
*Ready or not, here I come!* (hide-and-seek)
usageNote: **Hide** vs **conceal**: both mean to keep out of sight, but **conceal** is more formal and deliberate. **Hidden** as an adjective: *hidden agenda* (secret motives), *hidden gem* (an underappreciated gem of quality).

comparisons.description: to deliberately keep out of sight or knowledge — more formal than **hide**.
to conceal something undesirable or illegal; to suppress the truth.

idioms.phrase: hide one's light under a bushel
idioms.explanation: to conceal one's talents out of modesty — to not let others see your abilities.
to be completely transparent and honest; no secrets to conceal.

relatedForms.description: concealed; not immediately obvious or visible.
a secret place where someone hides, especially to avoid detection.

wordHistory: Old English *hydan* — to hide, to conceal. Proto-Indo-European root *skeu-* — to cover, to conceal. Cognate with German *hüten* (to guard).
contextStory: As a child he **hid** money in books — a few coins in each one. Once his mother found one, but **hid** her smile. He **has hidden** such 'treasures' all over the house. Now, as an adult, he understands: some things are better **hidden** — so that one day you can discover them again yourself.

forms: Past Simple: **hit**; Past Participle: **hit** (все три формы одинаковы: hit – hit – hit)

definitions.text: to strike someone or something with force; to make contact with: *the ball hit the window.*
examples: *She hit the nail on the head.*
*The car hit a tree and stopped.*

definitions.text: to suddenly come to mind; to affect suddenly and forcefully: *it suddenly hit me that I had forgotten.*
examples: *The reality of the situation finally hit him.*

definitions.text: a blow or impact; a successful song, film, or product: *the song became a hit.*

mainExamples: *He hit the target every single time.*
*The economic crisis has hit small businesses hardest.*
*Her new album hit number one within a week.*
usageNote: **Hit** is completely invariable: V1=V2=V3=**hit**. **Hit it off** = to immediately get along well with someone. **Hit the road** = to depart on a journey. **Hit the sack** = to go to bed.

comparisons.description: to hit — more formal and powerful; widely used in figurative contexts.
to hit repeatedly; to defeat someone — repeated blows or victory in competition.

idioms.phrase: hit the nail on the head
idioms.explanation: to describe or identify something exactly and accurately.
to begin a journey; to leave.
to immediately get along well and feel comfortable with someone.

relatedForms.description: uncompromisingly direct and forceful — used of journalism, criticism, and reports.

wordHistory: Middle English *hitten* — to strike, to reach. From Old Norse *hitta* — to meet, to hit upon. It replaced Old English *slean* (to strike) in most senses.
contextStory: He **hit** the ball with full force — and it **hit** the exact centre of the goal. The crowd erupted. A second later he was **hit** by realisation: this goal **has hit** him right in the heart — it was the winning strike in the final. He **hits** not just balls — he **hits** destiny.

forms: Past Simple: **held**; Past Participle: **held**

definitions.text: to keep something in your hand or arms; to support: *she held his hand tightly.*
examples: *He held the door open for her.*
*The dam held back the floodwaters.*

definitions.text: to organize or conduct an event: *the company held a meeting.*
examples: *Elections will be held in November.*

definitions.text: to have the capacity to contain: *the hall holds two thousand people.*
a grip; the cargo area of a ship: *get hold of* = to obtain; to contact someone.

mainExamples: *Hold your horses — don't rush into a decision.*
*She held her breath and jumped.*
*The world record has been held by the same athlete for ten years.*
usageNote: **Hold on** = wait; to grip tightly. **Hold back** = to restrain; to keep secret. **Hold up** = to delay; (informal) to rob. **Get hold of** = to obtain or contact.

comparisons.description: to seize or hold firmly — more sudden and intense than **hold**.
to hold tightly — emphasizes the strength of the hold.

idioms.explanation: to slow down or wait — don't act hastily.
to maintain one's position; not to be defeated in a difficult situation.
temporarily paused or suspended — said of a phone call or a project.

relatedForms.description: a person or object that holds something; the owner of a title or document.
a place or position that is difficult to take or give up; a fortress.

wordHistory: Old English *haldan* — to hold, to possess. Proto-Indo-European root *kel-* — to cover, to grasp. Cognate with German *halten*.
contextStory: She **held** his hand in the intensive care unit for three nights. The doctors said 'hang in there.' She **held** tighter. On the fourth day he **held** her gaze — and smiled. She **has held** that smile in her memory ever since. Sometimes all you can **hold** is someone's hand and a thread of hope.

forms: Past Simple: **kept**; Past Participle: **kept**

definitions.text: to retain something; to have something and not give it away: *keep the receipt.*
examples: *She kept all his letters for thirty years.*
*Keep your hands warm in winter.*

definitions.text: to continue doing something without stopping: *keep working.*
examples: *He kept talking even when nobody was listening.*
*She keeps forgetting my name.*

definitions.text: to honour or fulfil: *keep a promise.*
examples: *He always keeps his word.*

mainExamples: *Keep calm and carry on.*
*She kept the secret for twenty years.*
*He has kept his promise despite all difficulties.*
usageNote: **Keep + gerund** = to continue: *keep trying*. **Keep up** = to maintain pace; not to fall behind. **Keep in mind** = to remember; to bear in mind. **Keep to oneself** = to avoid sharing information; to be introverted.

comparisons.description: to keep in a certain state — more formal; often used for conditions, standards, and relationships.
to continue to have something — often used for rights, knowledge, or staff.

idioms.explanation: to maintain composure and not show distress during difficult times.
to try to match the social status and lifestyle of one's neighbours.
to stay cheerful and optimistic in difficult circumstances.

relatedForms.description: a person who guards or cares for something; a goalkeeper; something worth keeping.
the cost and effort of maintaining something in good condition.

wordHistory: Old English *cēpan* — to observe, to keep. Exact origin uncertain; possibly related to Latin *capere* (to take, to hold). Cognate with German *kümmern* (to care for).
contextStory: She **kept** his letters in a wooden box. She **kept** her promise never to show them to anyone. When she died, her granddaughter found the box — and **kept** it locked for another year. Then she opened it. Some things are **kept** not to be read — but simply so they **keep** those we love close.

forms: Past Simple: **knew**; Past Participle: **known**; Present Participle: **knowing**

definitions.text: to have information or facts in the mind: *do you know the answer?*
examples: *She knew every street in the old city.*
*He has known this family for thirty years.*

definitions.text: to be acquainted or familiar with someone or something: *I know her from university.*
examples: *Do you know how to drive?*

definitions.text: to be able to recognize: *I'd know his voice anywhere.*

mainExamples: *I know what I want, but I don't know how to get it.*
*She knew the moment she saw him that everything would change.*
*The truth has been known to them all along.*
usageNote: **Know** is a stative verb — not normally used in Continuous tenses: *I know* (not *I am knowing*). **Know how to** = to be able to do something. **As far as I know** = to the best of my knowledge. Colloquially, *you know?* is used as a filler.

comparisons.description: to grasp the meaning or significance of something — deeper than mere possession of facts.
to identify someone or something as familiar from previous experience.

idioms.explanation: to be experienced and skilled in a particular area; to understand how things work.
used to express uncertainty or indifference about something.
to know something thoroughly and in every detail.

relatedForms.description: facts, information, and skills acquired through experience or education.
familiar; recognised; established as fact.
not known or identified; something not yet discovered or understood.

wordHistory: Old English *cnawan* — to know, to recognise. Proto-Indo-European root *ĝneh₃-* — to know. Cognate with Latin *cognoscere* (the source of *cognition* and *recognize*), Greek *gignoskein*, and Russian *znat* (to know).
contextStory: She **knew** his secret — but never said a word. For years she **knew** and stayed silent. When he finally told her himself, she replied: "I **knew** it all along." He looked at her: "And you stayed anyway?" "Precisely because I **knew**," she smiled. True closeness begins where knowledge does not destroy.

forms: Past Simple: **led**; Past Participle: **led**

definitions.text: to guide or be in charge of; to go first: *she led her team to victory.*
examples: *He led the expedition through the jungle.*
*The path leads to the river.*

definitions.text: to cause or result in something: *hard work leads to success.*
examples: *One mistake led to a series of disasters.*

definitions.text: a position of advantage or leadership; a leash; an electrical wire; (homonym) a heavy grey metal: *take the lead.*

mainExamples: *Who will lead the country through this crisis?*
*She has led this organization for fifteen years.*
*All roads lead to Rome.*
usageNote: Common error: the past tense of **lead** is **led** [led], not *lead*. The noun *lead* has two pronunciations: [liːd] (leadership) and [led] (the metal) — two different words with the same spelling.

comparisons.description: to show the way, often with explanation and accompaniment — not necessarily in charge.
to administer and organise — not necessarily inspiring or going first.

idioms.explanation: to demonstrate the behaviour you expect from others through your own actions.

idioms.phrase: lead someone up the garden path
idioms.explanation: to deliberately mislead or deceive someone.

relatedForms.description: a person who leads others; the head of a group or organisation.
the ability to lead; the position or role of a leader.

wordHistory: Old English *lǣdan* — to lead, to guide. Proto-Indo-European root *leit-* — to go forward. Cognate with German *leiten* (to direct, to guide).
contextStory: He **led** the group through the mountain pass in a snowstorm. No one knew the way except him. He **led** in silence, step by step. By evening, he **had led** everyone to shelter. "How did you **lead** us in such darkness?" someone asked. "I simply knew where fear **leads** — and walked the other way."

forms: Past Simple: **left**; Past Participle: **left**

definitions.text: to go away from a place or person: *she left the room without a word.*
examples: *The train leaves at seven.*
*He left home when he was eighteen.*

definitions.text: to allow something to remain; to place something for someone to find: *leave a message.*
examples: *I left my keys on the table.*
*Leave the children with the babysitter.*

definitions.text: an authorised absence from work or duty: *maternity leave; sick leave.*

mainExamples: *He left without saying goodbye — and she never forgave him.*
*She had left her old life behind completely.*
*Leave it to me — I'll handle it.*
usageNote: **Leave** vs **let**: *leave* = to physically depart or allow something to remain; *let* = to permit. **Leave alone** = to stop bothering someone. **Leave behind** = to not take with you. **Take leave of** = to say goodbye to.

comparisons.description: to leave a place — more formal and ceremonious than **leave**.
to leave permanently and irresponsibly; to desert.

idioms.explanation: to try every possible means to find or achieve something.
to make a lasting impression or impact on something or someone.
to avoid interfering with something that is working adequately.

relatedForms.description: food remaining after a meal; something remaining from a previous time.

wordHistory: Old English *lǣfan* — to leave behind, related to *lāf* (remainder). Proto-Indo-European root *leip-* — to stick, to remain. Cognate with German *bleiben* (to remain, to stay).
contextStory: He **left** her at the station — with one suitcase and her aunt's address. She **left** everything: the city, the past, the name. The train pulled away, and she **left** the window fogged with tears. But something inside her **left** fear behind. Sometimes, to **leave** — you simply have to find the courage.

forms: Past Simple: **let**; Past Participle: **let** (все три формы одинаковы: let – let – let)

definitions.text: to allow or permit someone to do something: *let me help you.*
examples: *Her parents let her stay out late.*
*Let me know when you arrive.*

definitions.text: (British English) to rent out property: *they let their cottage for the summer.*
examples: *Room to let.*

definitions.text: used to form the first-person imperative (let's): *let's go!*
examples: *Let's not argue about this.*

mainExamples: *Let him speak — he deserves to be heard.*
*She let the opportunity slip through her fingers.*
*They had let the situation get out of control.*
usageNote: **Let** + object + bare infinitive (no *to*): *let me do it* (not *let me to do it*). **Let** vs **allow**: both mean to permit, but **let** is more colloquial. **Let alone** = not to mention. **Let go** = to release.

comparisons.description: to permit — more formal than **let**, often used for official authorisation.
to give formal authorisation — most official of the three, used in rules and laws.

idioms.phrase: let the cat out of the bag
idioms.explanation: to accidentally reveal a secret that was supposed to be kept hidden.
to avoid bringing up old problems or conflicts; don't stir up trouble.
to release pent-up energy, anger, or emotion.

relatedForms.description: a disappointment — something that fails to meet expectations.

wordHistory: Old English *lǣtan* — to allow, to leave. Proto-Indo-European root *lēd-* — to be slow, to delay. Cognate with German *lassen* (to let, to leave, to allow).
contextStory: His father **let** him fall — deliberately. The boy splashed in the water until he swam on his own. "Why did you **let** me drown?" he asked later. "I **let** you learn," his father answered. "I would never **let** you actually drown." Sometimes the greatest act of love is choosing to **let** go.

forms: Past Simple: **lost**; Past Participle: **lost**

definitions.text: to no longer have something because you cannot find it or it has been taken: *I lost my wallet on the bus.*
examples: *She lost her job during the pandemic.*
*He lost his way in the forest.*

definitions.text: to fail to win a contest or argument: *our team lost the match.*
examples: *He lost the argument badly.*

definitions.text: to be bereaved of someone close: *she lost her mother last year.*

mainExamples: *He lost everything in the fire but started over.*
*You lose some, you win some.*
*She has lost track of how many times she tried.*
usageNote: Common error: **lose** (verb) vs **loose** (adjective, meaning not tight). *Don't lose this key* vs *a loose fit*. **Get lost** = to become disoriented; (informal, rude) go away.

comparisons.description: to temporarily lose something by putting it in the wrong place — less final than **lose**.
to lose something as a penalty or consequence of breaking a rule.

idioms.explanation: to suddenly become very angry and lose self-control.
to become discouraged and give up hope.
to be in a situation where any risk is justified because the alternative is no better.

relatedForms.description: the fact of losing something or someone; a financial deficit; grief.
unable to find the way; no longer possessed; irretrievably gone.
a person who loses; (informal) someone regarded as a failure.

wordHistory: Old English *losian* — to be lost, to perish. Proto-Indo-European root *leu-* — to cut, to separate. Related to Latin *luere* (to wash away, to pay a penalty).
contextStory: He **lost** his wedding ring a month after the ceremony. She **lost** hope of ever finding it a year later. But one day the gardener **lost** his balance and overturned a flower bed — and there, in the soil, lay the ring. He **has lost** many things in this life. But some things are **lost** only to return.

forms: Past Simple: **made**; Past Participle: **made**

definitions.text: to form, construct, or create something: *she made a cake.*
examples: *He made a table from old wood.*
*The company has made significant progress.*

definitions.text: to cause someone to feel something or to do something: *the news made her cry.*
examples: *Music makes me happy.*
*He made her feel special.*

definitions.text: to earn money: *she makes a good salary.*

mainExamples: *He made a decision that changed his life.*
*She made her way through the crowd.*
*We have made so many memories together.*
usageNote: Key distinction: **make** vs **do**. **Make** = to create a product: *make a mistake, make a decision, make a sandwich*. **Do** = to perform an activity: *do homework, do business, do a favour*. There is no strict rule — collocations must be learned individually.

comparisons.description: to perform an activity — does not create a product, focuses on the process.
to bring something original into existence — more artistic and elevated than **make**.
to manufacture, especially in large quantities.

idioms.explanation: to have just enough money to pay for necessities; to barely get by financially.
to reach a decision; to decide.
to use something to the best possible advantage.

relatedForms.description: a person or company that makes something; a creator.
a transformation of someone's appearance or style.

wordHistory: Old English *macian* — to make, to construct. Proto-Indo-European root *mag-* — to be able, to fit. Cognate with German *machen* (to make, to do).
contextStory: She **made** her first dress from old curtains at age eight. By twenty she **made** a collection for a fashion house. Now her work **makes** people weep on the runway — from sheer beauty. "What do you **make** these from?" journalists ask. "From everything that **made** me who I am."

forms: Past Simple: **meant**; Past Participle: **meant**

definitions.text: to have as a meaning; to express or intend to convey: *what does this word mean?*
examples: *I didn't mean to hurt you.*
*What do you mean by that?*

definitions.text: to intend to do something: *I mean to finish this today.*
examples: *She meant well but caused more problems.*

definitions.text: unkind or cruel; (informal) not generous with money: *a mean trick.*
examples: *Don't be mean to your sister.*

mainExamples: *She meant every word she said.*
*This promotion has meant the world to him.*
*He meant to call her, but kept forgetting.*
usageNote: **Mean** + infinitive = to intend (*I mean to go*). **Mean** + gerund = to have as a consequence (*this means waiting longer*). **It means a lot** = it is very important. *I mean...* is a common conversational filler.

comparisons.description: to mean — more formal, typically used for symbols, signs, and important indicators.
to plan or have in mind — a more formal synonym for the 'have a plan' sense.
